Early Middle Stone Age personal ornaments from Bizmoune Cave, Essaouira, Morocco
El Mehdi Sehasseh , Philippe Fernandez , Steven Kuhn , Mary Stiner , Susan Mentzer , Debra Colarossi , Amy Clark , François Lanoe, Matthew Pailes , [...] , and Abdeljalil Bouzouggar +16 authors
 Authors Info & AffiliationsScience Advances22 Sep 2021Vol 7, Issue 39DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.abi8620Notifications

Abstract
Ornaments such as beads are among the earliest signs of symbolic behavior among human ancestors. Their appearance signals important developments in both cognition and social relations. This paper describes and presents contextual information for 33 shell beads from Bizmoune Cave (southwest Morocco). Many of the beads come as deposits dating to ≥142 thousand years, making them the oldest shell beads yet recovered. They extend the dates for the first appearance of this behavior into the late Middle Pleistocene. The ages and ubiquity of beads in Middle Stone Age (MSA) sites in North Africa provide further evidence of the potential importance of these artifacts as signals of identity. failure_log.content: The webpage details the discovery of 33 perforated marine shells, primarily *Tritia gibbosula*, from Bizmoune Cave in Essaouira, Morocco, dating back to at least 142,000 years ago—making them the oldest known shell beads in the world. These personal ornaments, found in Early Middle Stone Age (MSA) layers, were likely used as symbolic items and demonstrate early human cognitive complexity and social signaling. The shells, some showing signs of human modification, wear, and traces of red ochre, were transported from the coast, indicating deliberate collection and use. This discovery highlights the cultural continuity and symbolic behavior of early *Homo sapiens* in North Africa and pushes back the timeline for the emergence of symbolic material culture into the late Middle Pleistocene.

Based on the research article published in *Science Advances* in 2021 titled "Early Middle Stone Age personal ornaments from Bizmoune Cave, Essaouira, Morocco," the shell beads were made from the mollusk species *Tritia gibbosula* (formerly known as *Nassarius gibbosulus*). The abstract of this article states that these beads are at least **142,000 years old**.

Therefore, beads made from the shells of this species were found that are at least **142 thousand years old**.
